content:
Hey, for the Security Module I'm developing for my master's thesis (see old thread here for context) I need a way to store the policy, some additional data (similar to SELinux's persistent label mapping) and also caching data globally.

Currently I'm using the global_lock! macro together with KVecs for the storage, but since the policy and additional data only need to be read in most cases, using a mutex and fully locking them for each policy decision feels pretty inefficient. I looked into other options in the kernel and found either rw_locks or RCU, but neither seem to be fully implemented in Rust? Please correct me if I'm wrong.

For data storage I'm using KVecs at the moment (might look into Lists to limit places of possible allocations), but much data (additional information, caching) would more naturally fit in a HashMap. Is anyone working on bindings or an implementation for that? I thought about trying to port hashbrown to the kernel, not sure if that would be worth doing or if it's a bad fit.

I know I'm conflating the locking and HashMap topic into one here, but I read in the RCU docs that data structures need to support it, so I felt that the topics are somewhat related.

As for storing the data in other places, I know LSMs can use the security fields in some structs, but those do not work with LSM stacking and I have not looked too deeply into the state of the blobs that are supposed to enable stacking in all cases. At least for the policy resolution cache I will need to store something in the LSM itself anyway, similar to how SELinux's access vector cache is stored in a static variable.

Is there a WIP patch for rw_locks/RCU/HashMaps that I could cherry-pick? Or do you have other suggestions what I could look into?
There aren't bindings for this.
Ok, so at least I didn't miss anything on the mailing list, thanks. I guess my options are then to either build things myself if I have the time or live with what's there.
I have some of the RCU protected pointer wrapper in my atomic patchset: https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20241101060237.1185533-14-boqun.feng@gmail.com/
It doesn't have call_rcu() support though

Thanks! I don't think the missing call_rcu() is a problem. But I don't think I can use this to protect data I'm storing in a static variable though, can I? Rcu<P> is only Sync where P: Sync, whereas the other Lock implementations/backends only require a Send bound to be Sync. And how would I acquire the Pin<&mut Self> to exchange the content in that case?

Also, to get the patchset (with v2.1 applied) to compile on v6.14-rc6 I had to remove two calls to cast_mut() because ForeignOwnable::into_foreign() already returns *mut crate::ffi::c_void:

diff --git a/rust/kernel/sync/rcu.rs b/rust/kernel/sync/rcu.rs
index 8326b2e0986a..da96f732486f 100644
--- a/rust/kernel/sync/rcu.rs
+++ b/rust/kernel/sync/rcu.rs
@@ -95,7 +95,7 @@ impl<P: ForeignOwnable> Rcu<P> {
     pub fn new(p: P) -> Self {
         // INVARIANTS: The return value of `p.into_foreign()` is directly stored in the atomic
         // variable.
-        Self(Atomic::new(p.into_foreign().cast_mut()), PhantomData)
+        Self(Atomic::new(p.into_foreign()), PhantomData)
     }

     /// Dereferences the protected object.
@@ -243,7 +243,7 @@ pub fn read_copy_update<F>(self: Pin<&mut Self>, f: F) -> Option<RcuOld<P>>

         // step 3: UPDATE.
         if let Some(new) = new {
-            let new_ptr = new.into_foreign().cast_mut();
+            let new_ptr = new.into_foreign();
             // Ordering: Pairs with the address dependency in `dereference()` and
             // `read_copy_update()`.
             // INVARIANTS: `new.into_foreign()` is directly store into the atomic variable.
because it's similar to rwlock: multiple reader could exists, so it's Sync only if T is Sync: https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/sync/struct.RwLock.html#impl-Sync-for-RwLock%3CT%3E
(deleted)
and yes, your adjustment for v6.14-rc6 seems valid, I have the same thing locally.

Ah, so my issue to do something like this for example:

static POLICY: Rcu<KBox<Policy>> = Rcu::new(KBox::new(Policy::new(), GFP_KERNEL)?);

is that I'm missing something like LazyLock to handle the initialization?
what is the error you got?

Because my current code, using the global_lock, looks like this:

global_lock! {
    // SAFETY: Initialized in module initializer before first use.
    unsafe(uninit) static POLICY: Mutex<Policy> = Policy::new();
}

// Called by the main LSM init function
pub(crate) fn init() -> Result<()> {
    // SAFETY: Called exactly once.
    unsafe { POLICY.init() };
...
}

and then later when the LSM hook gets called I evaluate the policy rules using POLICY.lock(), same in the update function for the policy.

Boqun Feng said:

what is the error you got?

error[E0572]: return statement outside of function body
  --> security/lsm/pdp.rs:21:81
   |
21 | static POLICY: Rcu<KBox<Policy>> = Rcu::new(KBox::new(Policy::new(), GFP_KERNEL)?);
   |                                                                                 ^

error: aborting due to 1 previous error

Since I guess I can't allocate or deal with the Result in the const context.
global_lock needs extra init steps because we haven't supported const initialization of locks (due to we need an FFI to initialize)
anyway, I think it might be easier for your case to start with a rwlock.
because to use RCU, your data structure also need to be somewhat Sync (i.e. having interior mutability)

Boqun Feng said:

global_lock needs extra init steps because we haven't supported const initialization of locks (due to we need an FFI to initialize)

That's fine though and works for me, I'm looking for a way to replace it with something RCU-protected, since the policy only gets read in most cases.

Boqun Feng said:

anyway, I think it might be easier for your case to start with a rwlock.

That sounds like it makes more sense then, but there are no bindings for that, are there?
No, but it shouldn't be hard to write one (famous last words)
what does your data structure look like?

Sorry, I just had a meeting with my advisor for the thesis.

Boqun Feng said:

what does your data structure look like?

The main struct I'm storing is

#[derive(Debug)]
pub(crate) struct Policy {
    pub(crate) rules: KVec<Rule>,
}

Through a few other other structs the whole structure (underneath Rule) contains mostly primitive data types and KVecs, some enums and a CString once (which I'm probably replacing by an integer id soon).

In some places I'm using KVecs even though a HashMap would be more fitting, but since there is none yet this was a way to get something working first and then improve it later.

I also wanted to add caching later, similar to the access vector cache SELinux uses (to store already evaluated policy decisions and respond faster), but since SELinux uses a spinlock for that, that seems like the way to go for that. The bigger problem for that is the missing HashMap to use as a cache.

If I try to create bindings for rwlock, would that work as another Backend for kernel::sync::lock::Lock? Since that only requires a lock operation, but does not differentiate between locking to read and locking to write.

Lukas Fischer said:

If I try to create bindings for rwlock, would that work as another Backend for kernel::sync::lock::Lock? Since that only requires a lock operation, but does not differentiate between locking to read and locking to write.

I think Backend and kernel::sync::lock::Lock are more designed for exclusive locks, you probably want to start with a simple wrapper for rwlock_t or rwsemphore.

Boqun Feng said:

I think Backend and kernel::sync::lock::Lock are more designed for exclusive locks, you probably want to start with a simple wrapper for rwlock_t or rwsemphore.

Seems reasonable.

I was reading the docs on the rwlocks though, and for rwlock_t I saw:

NOTE! reader-writer locks require more atomic memory operations than simple spinlocks. Unless the reader critical section is long, you are better off just using spinlocks.

So it seems like I might be trying to prematurely optimize something I haven't even measured yet, just because it seems like it would make sense from the access pattern.

NOTE! We are working hard to remove reader-writer spinlocks in most cases, so please don’t add a new one without consensus. (Instead, see RCU Concepts for complete information.)

And it also seems as if rwlock_t is not something most people would want to add/use anyway.

Boqun Feng said:

because to use RCU, your data structure also need to be somewhat Sync (i.e. having interior mutability)

I actually thought an RCU wrapper would provide this interior mutability and then more or less provide functions to acquire a read lock and to replace the stored value. But I guess that is too inflexible for some data structures.
First, normally RCU needs to pair with an exclusive lock (e.g. spinlock) as the update locking. That part is yet not supported by the current locking API.
Second, say you have a Rcu<KBox<Policy>>, you can replace the whole KBox<Policy> with Rcu::replace(), but that means whenever you do the update, you need to copy the whole vector.
unless your vector supports RCU update.

Boqun Feng said:

Second, say you have a Rcu<KBox<Policy>>, you can replace the whole KBox<Policy> with Rcu::replace(), but that means whenever you do the update, you need to copy the whole vector.

I knew about that, but I guess I didn't really think about the consequences of that. Currently my WIP LSM allocates all over the place anyway, but I'll remove allocations from the policy resolution path, where it gets called for access decisions.

I'm not too set on using vectors, lists would work as well, but it seems like ones with RCU support still need quite a bit of work. Looking at the previous discussions about LKMM in Rust and the related topics, I don't think I can meaningfully contribute to that, sorry.
No problem. I'm working on making it more usable ;-)
Maybe I could provide a RCU protected list soon

I'll look into maybe adding bindings for an rwlock and I'll try different locking variants during the evaluation phase of my thesis. I need to hand it in at the end of May, so everything that isn't available before then will then become an answer to the "limitations of the current Linux kernel and Rust environment" research question.
Thanks for your help :)
Lukas Fischer has marked this topic as resolved.
Just keep me posted, if I can look into your use cases, I may be able to provide the locking mechanism that you may need.

Use cases are more or less:

  • store the policy, which means a collection of rules storing details about how to provide access (exact policy semantics are part of unpublished research, so I can't be super specific with this, sorry, I can talk to my advisor @Peter Amthor if you need more details)
  • store some additional data for policy evaluation with a user/object to data mapping (feels like a HashMap would be nice there, but that'S also not available yet and especially not with RCU support), this could also be stored using the security context pointers or the lsm blobs but I need to look more into them
  • store caching information to speed up policy resolution, which is also a map from user and object (or security context if you want to think in SELinux terms) to access decision (bool)

The policy and information storage mostly only get read, sometimes updated, how often and how that impacts the performance will be part of my evaluation. The cache gets updated every time an access decision is made. I checked the SELinux code again, and saw that they do actually use RCU for that, with a spinlock to synchronize the writes.

And of course I don't need to store all this in static variables, I just couldn't think of a different place for it, since the lsm hooks themselves only provide the data structures necessary for each access decision. Those do contain the security fields for LSMs, but they aren't meant to store the whole policy.

This seems work, a few things to notice:

  • I didn't quite understand your question about LazyLock, but it turns out that Rcu should have a const initializer, hence I added Rcu::null().
  • Projectable is an idea for things that can have multiple immutable references and one Pin<&mut>, this is what RCU needs.
  • ProjectableGlobalLockedBy is similar to normal GlobalLockedBy except that you cannot get an &mut T, because swap() then will ruin the atomicity for accessing the RCU pointer.
  • Note that every time you update Policy, you need to copy the whole existing vector, this is because vector itself is not an RCU-aware data structure. But the copy may not be that bad, because readers can still read the old data, until you update it.

Boqun Feng said:

This seems work

Amazing, that is more or less what I was looking for. I've rebased my commits on top of yours, works as it's supposed to.

  • I didn't quite understand your question about LazyLock, but it turns out that Rcu should have a const initializer, hence I added Rcu::null().

Yeah I think that's what I was missing there, a const initializer that makes it usable in static variables. I saw that the standard library uses the Lazy types for statics that need allocations or initialization, so I went with that as an example.

  • Note that every time you update Policy, you need to copy the whole existing vector, this is because vector itself is not an RCU-aware data structure. But the copy may not be that bad, because readers can still read the old data, until you update it.

The only way the policy can get updated is through an interface in securityfs, where a new policy is loaded from userspace. This means it builds a completely new policy from parsing that input anyway, my previous code also only built a new policy object and replaced the one stored in the Mutex. So allocation-wise there's no difference there.

As part of my evaluation I compared using SpinLocks vs Mutexes for other data which I cannot store using RCU, but since I was already setting up my code for a simple switch between both, I also switched between a Mutex and a SpinLock for the GlobalLockBackend of the ProjectableGlobalLockedBy. That's when I first noticed, that using a SpinLock for that results in a kernel panic with scheduling while atomic.

This is more or less what I'm using:

global_lock! {
    // SAFETY: Initialized in module initializer before first use.
    unsafe(uninit) static POLICY_WRITE_GUARD: Mutex<()> = ();
}

static POLICY: ProjectableGlobalLockedBy<Rcu<KBox<Policy>>, POLICY_WRITE_GUARD> =
    ProjectableGlobalLockedBy::new(Rcu::null());

// SAFETY: All initializers are called exactly once.
unsafe {
    POLICY_WRITE_GUARD.init();
}

pub(crate) fn set_policy(policy: Policy) -> Result {
    let policy = KBox::new(policy, GFP_KERNEL)?;
    let mut guard = POLICY_WRITE_GUARD.lock();
    let mut policy_writer = POLICY.as_mut(&mut guard);
    policy_writer.as_mut().replace(policy);

    Ok(())
}

Replacing the Mutex in the global_lock! with SpinLock results in the panic, with the Mutex it works. I don't think I'm holding any other locks or using the RCU otherwise in the meantime, as it also does this when this is the very first thing I do with my LSM after booting the kernel. This is not a show stopper for me as I can just keep using a Mutex for my RCU protected data, as that's not part of the case where switching to SpinLocks is actually interesting to look at, but I figured this might be something you want to look at, maybe RCU just is not meant to be used with SpinLocks and I overlooked that?

FYI I'm still using the version you initially published, I saw that you rebased and force pushed the dev/rust/atomic-v3 branch, but I'm still on the stable releases of v6.14 so that I'm not using an rc for the thesis in the end.
If you get a scheduling while atomic inside the spinlock, then you're doing something that sleeps. I don't know what it is, though. RCU shouldn't do that on its own.

I've been trying to figure out what it could be that sleeps, but I can't find anything, from the moment I enter into my code through my securityfs implementation, I'm doing nothing else but reading all the data from user space using a UserSlice, converting it to a Policy object using str::parse and then calling the function I posted with that.

Since I'm not doing anything else, other kernel threads shouldn't do anything in my LSM either, and even if, shouldn't that not matter with atomics?

Here's how the data ends up there:

#[no_mangle]
unsafe extern "C" fn thesis_lsm_update_policy(
    _file: *mut bindings::file,
    ptr: UserPtr,
    length: c_ulong,
    _offset: *mut c_longlong,
) -> c_int {
    fs_update_policy(ptr, length)
}

// not the nicest interface since it ignores the offset, but works for now
pub(crate) fn fs_update_policy(
    ptr: UserPtr,
    length: c_ulong,
) -> c_int {
    let mut buf = KVec::new();

    if let Err(e) = UserSlice::new(ptr, length).read_all(&mut buf, GFP_KERNEL) {
        return e.to_errno();
    }

    if let Err(e) = target(&buf) {
        return e.to_errno();
    }

    buf.len() as _
}

pub(crate) fn update_policy(policy: &[u8]) -> Result {
    // Read and parse the policy and update it
    let policy: Policy = str::from_utf8(policy)?.parse()?;
    pr_info!("Updating policy to: {policy:?}");
    set_policy(policy)
}

The first function is the one registered under the dentry in the securityfs, the last one calls the set_policy function from above.
Doesn't the kernel panic have a backtrace?
Ah true, wait

It looks like set_policy calls synchronize_rcu which you definitely can't do while holding a spinlock.
I'm not manually calling it, so I guess it's coming from the WIP RCU implementation.
No, it's a call inside set_policy.
hmm
or maybe it's from the krealloc call

pub(crate) fn set_policy(policy: Policy) -> Result {
    let policy = KBox::new(policy, GFP_KERNEL)?;
    let mut guard = POLICY_WRITE_GUARD.lock();
    let mut policy_writer = POLICY.as_mut(&mut guard);
    policy_writer.as_mut().replace(policy);

    Ok(())
}

But set_policy is just this
right
There it is.
You can't allocate memory with GFP_KERNEL under a spinlock.
But I thought I'm taking the spinlock after the allocation there
There isn't a spinlock around the call to set_policy?
No, the spinlock is taken with let mut guard = POLICY_WRITE_GUARD.lock();
What is replace?

That's from Boqun's RCU branch:

pub fn replace(self: Pin<&mut Self>, new: P) -> Option<RcuOld<P>> {
    self.read_copy_update(|_| Some(new))
}
Which is why I'm wondering if maybe that quick WIP implementation he made for me to support global locks does not work with spinlocks.
And read_copy_update?

pub fn read_copy_update<F>(self: Pin<&mut Self>, f: F) -> Option<RcuOld<P>>
    where
        F: FnOnce(Option<P::Borrowed<'_>>) -> Option<P>,
    {
        // step 1: READ.
        // Ordering: Address dependency pairs with the `store(Release)` in read_copy_update().
        let old_ptr = NonNull::new(self.0.load(Relaxed));

        let old = old_ptr.map(|nonnull| {
            // SAFETY: Per type invariants `old_ptr` has to be a value return by a previous
            // `into_foreign()`, and the exclusive reference `self` guarantees that `from_foreign()`
            // has not been called.
            unsafe { P::borrow(nonnull.as_ptr()) }
        });

        // step 2: COPY, or more generally, initializing `new` based on `old`.
        let new = f(old);

        // step 3: UPDATE.
        if let Some(new) = new {
            let new_ptr = new.into_foreign();
            // Ordering: Pairs with the address dependency in `dereference()` and
            // `read_copy_update()`.
            // INVARIANTS: `new.into_foreign()` is directly store into the atomic variable.
            self.0.store(new_ptr, Release);
        } else {
            // Ordering: Setting to a null pointer doesn't need to be Release.
            // INVARIANTS: The atomic variable is set to be null.
            self.0.store(core::ptr::null_mut(), Relaxed);
        }

        // INVARIANTS: The exclusive reference guarantess that the ownership of a previous
        // `into_foreign()` transferred to the `RcuOld`.
        Some(RcuOld(old_ptr?, PhantomData))
    }

Are there calls to synchronize_rcu() in any of the destructors of those rcu pointers?
drop Rcu<P> pointer will call synchronize_rcu
Problem may at the policy_writer.as_mut().replace()? You can disassemble and check

JCKeep said:

drop Rcu<P> pointer will call synchronize_rcu

Yeah the point of the read_copy_update function is to safely change the value protected by RCU, so it makes sense it would call it there. The Lock is needed to ensure that only one process at a time can modify the value. Since I needed to store RCU protected data in a static variable, Boqun added a few commits making it work with global_lock by using ProjectableGlobalLockedBy. Since I need to hold the lock to call read_copy_update because it guarantees I am the only thread doing so, I cannot unlock it until after the call, but by then synchronize_rcu() is called at the end of that call. Which all seems to me like this API just cannot work with SpinLocks.

Here's this part of the function with types inserted:

let mut guard: kernel::sync::GlobalGuard<POLICY_WRITE_GUARD> = POLICY_WRITE_GUARD.lock();
let mut policy_writer: Pin<&mut Rcu<Box<Policy, kernel::alloc::allocator::Cmalloc>>> = POLICY.as_mut(&mut guard);
policy_writer.as_mut().read_copy_update(|_| Some(policy));

As I said, I'll just always use Mutexes with RCU for now.

Most likely you can do 

pub(crate) fn set_policy(policy: Policy) -> Result {
    // Defined before `guard` to drop after releasing spinlock.
    let old;
    let policy = KBox::new(policy, GFP_KERNEL)?;
    let mut guard = POLICY_WRITE_GUARD.lock();
    let mut policy_writer = POLICY.as_mut(&mut guard);
    old = policy_writer.as_mut().replace(policy);

    Ok(())
}
